text:
10 BOOKS SERIOUS AI RESEARCHERS ACTUALLY RECOMMEND (NOT THE ONES EVERYONE POSTS)

Every AI reading list says the same five names. The people actually building these systems read deeper than that. Here's the shelf they point to when nobody's performing for an audience.

1. Probability Theory: The Logic of Science - E.T. Jaynes

The book researchers quietly call life-changing. Jaynes reframes probability not as gambling odds but as the mathematics of reasoning under uncertainty, which is exactly what every modern model is doing. Dense, opinionated, and the closest thing the field has to a sacred text. Almost nobody outside the work has heard of it.

2. Information Theory, Inference, and Learning Algorithms - David MacKay

The book that unites information theory and machine learning in one place, written by a Cambridge physicist who made it genuinely fun. Free online, full of puzzles, and on the shelf of nearly every researcher who actually understands why their models compress and predict the way they do.

3. Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction - Sutton and Barto

The foundation under everything from AlphaGo to how modern models get fine-tuned with human feedback. Researchers don't recommend it because it's trendy. They recommend it because the ideas in it keep turning out to be the ideas that matter, decades later. Also free.

4. The Book of Why - Judea Pearl

A Turing Award winner's argument that today's AI is stuck because it confuses correlation with causation, and a map of what real reasoning would require. The book that names the exact ceiling current systems keep hitting. Researchers cite it constantly. The public reads past it.

5. Vision - David Marr

A neuroscientist's framework for how any system, brain or machine, processes information, written before deep learning existed and somehow predicting the questions it would face. The "levels of analysis" idea in here quietly shapes how serious people think about what a model is even doing.

6. Gödel, Escher, Bach - Douglas Hofstadter

The cult book about how meaning and selfhood emerge from systems following simple rules. It won a Pulitzer and then got name-dropped to death, but almost nobody finishes it. The ones who do think differently about intelligence forever. The real one, not the summary.

7. Metaphors We Live By - Lakoff and Johnson

The argument that human thought runs on metaphor, not cold logic, and that you can't build a mind on first-order logic alone. Researchers working on why language models grasp meaning the strange way they do keep circling back to this one. A genuine left-field pick.

8. The Society of Mind - Marvin Minsky

One of AI's founding figures arguing that intelligence isn't one thing, it's a swarm of dumb little processes working together. Written as hundreds of one-page ideas. Out of fashion for years, now looking prophetic in the age of multi-agent systems. Insider catnip.

9. How to Solve It - George Pólya

A 1945 book on mathematical problem-solving that quietly shaped how a generation of researchers think about breaking down hard problems, and that keeps surfacing in papers on how to make models reason. The bridge between human heuristics and machine reasoning.

10. The Mathematical Theory of Communication - Claude Shannon

The original paper that invented information theory and, with it, the entire conceptual ground that machine learning stands on. Short, brutal, and foundational. Researchers revere Shannon the way physicists revere Newton. Most reading lists skip the source and quote the descendants.

The popular books tell you what AI might do. These tell you how the people building it actually think. The difference is the whole point.

People are earning up to $40/hr training AI models remotely for major tech companies? 

... and there are over 10 platforms like Outlier currently accepting Nigerians.

You may not know this, but Big tech companies like OpenAI, Google, and Meta are quietly paying ordinary people $15–$50/hr to train their AI models from home.

Most people have no idea this exists. Let me break it down. 👇

OpenAI. Google. Meta. Anthropic.

These companies are building the most powerful AI systems in history.

And they can't do it without you.

Literally.

Here's what they don't advertise:

AI models don't just "learn" on their own.

They need humans to label images, rank responses, write prompts, evaluate outputs, and teach them right from wrong.

That job is called Data Annotation. And it pays in dollars.

Data Annotation = labeling raw data (text, images, audio, video) so AI understands the world.

Think of it as being a teacher for machines.

You tell the AI: "this answer is better than that one." "This image contains a dog." "This code has a bug."

It learns. You get paid.

The global AI training data market is on track to hit $17 billion by 2030.

Companies are desperately hiring human annotators right now - not next year. Now.

And the beautiful part? You don't need a degree. Just a laptop, good English, and critical thinking.

Here's the breakdown of what you can earn:

🟢 General tasks → $15–$25/hr
🟡 Writing/research tasks → $20–$35/hr
🔴 Expert tasks (coding, medicine, law) → $40–$100/hr

No experience. No certificate. No portfolio. Just your brain.

There are multiple platforms for this — including ones that work for Africans.

Some of the biggest names don't accept us. But several do — and pay just as well.

We've built the complete manual to navigate this space safely. Inside the guide, you will get:

→ Every platform worth your time
→ Which ones accept Nigerians or people in your country.
→ How to sign up and pass qualification tests
→ How to receive your pay in Naira
→ How to avoid scam platforms
→ What job you're required to do and how to do it.

See the link in comment section to Learn More or get the full guide and start your setup today!

My entire AI SEO content writing workflow 👇

(Use this to CRUSH your competitors)

1️⃣ Research phase

→ Scrape the top 10-20 results for your keyword
→ Keep the posts with real data and a real angle, ditch the thin sales pages and the outdated junk
→ Deep-read the top 3-5: word count, heading structure, which sections go deep, the exact data points they use
→ Note what they internally link to
→ Map the table stakes - every topic that shows up in 3+ of the top posts.
→ Then do the gap analysis: what do they ALL leave out? The question nobody answers, the comparison nobody runs, the data nobody has. That's your reason to outrank them

2️⃣ SEO foundation

→ Slug = your keyword with dashes, no year
→ Title tag, 50-60 chars, keyword up front
→ Meta description, 150-160 chars, with a number + a reason to click
→ Word count target = match or beat the average of the top 3

3️⃣ Build the outline

→ Every H2 and H3, in order
→ One line per section on what it covers + the exact data/range/example to drop in
→ Flag where a table or chart makes it stronger

4️⃣  Content writing

→ In your brand voice (we/our, never third person)
→ Problem-agitate-solution intro, 50-80 words
→ 3-5 PRIMARY sources only, linked inline (no "top 10 stats" roundups)
→ 5-10 internal links to your service + related pages, with 2-3 word descriptive anchors
→ Short paragraphs, tables for anything with 3+ options
→ 4-5 ideas for visuals, each with alt text + a copy-paste AI image prompt
→ Key takeaways box, then 9-11 FAQs written for snippets + People Also Ask

5️⃣ The human part:

→ QA every draft for hallucinations and made-up stats
→ Add real screenshots, images, and custom graphics
→ Make sure the internal links actually make sense
→ Add the insight and examples AI just doesn't have
→ Double-check every source actually says what you claim
→ Read it out loud once - if it sounds like a robot, rewrite it
→ Publish, then make sure it's indexed and linked from related posts

Today, we're excited to release DFD (Data Forcing Distillation), a single-line fix that restores diversity and fidelity in DMD for few-step video distillation, led by my PhD student @SiyiChen277112! It improves on text-to-video, image-to-video, and causal auto-regressive video generation. 

The idea is simple: directly introducing real video samples into the training objective during distillation, so that the student can be explicitly pulled toward the diverse, high-quality real data distribution. This is implemented as a single line of code change when computing teacher scores: instead of evaluating the teacher score at the student's own generation (original DMD), we evaluate it on a real sample and compute the discrepancy with the fake sample. This pulls the student toward modes it missed (mitigating mode collapse) and away from over-saturated modes absent in real data.

With this single-line change, we can purely discard the GAN loss, and restore the diversity/fidelity by finetuning the DMD checkpoint with only 100–300 steps. 

Everything is released. Code (including both inference & training), pretrained model, and data:
Paper: https://t.co/GyWdjZ1Gah
Project page: https://t.co/8xnKb3vTKY
Code, model, and data: https://t.co/WGyo0kPMb1

We also want to have a huge shout-out to the authors of FastGen (https://t.co/SnPsjXLPX4) and Self-Forcing (https://t.co/KN3nli41oG) for releasing their code, which makes our work possible!

#VideoGeneration #DiffusionModels #AI #OpenSource

AI app ideas are cheap. Launchable templates are the bottleneck.

Awesome Generative AI Apps is a curated GitHub repo of 50+ open-source generative AI app templates for builders who want to clone, deploy, and adapt AI SaaS products.

It helps you skip the blank Next.js project by grouping templates for image, video, beauty/fashion, e-commerce, writing, agents, audio, and integrations, with setup and deploy paths already documented.

Key features:

• 50+ app templates – cloneable products across image generation, video generation, virtual try-on, product photography, content, agents, and more
• SaaS plumbing included – README highlights Stripe billing, Google OAuth, Prisma/PostgreSQL, credit balances, and job history
• Vercel deployment path – templates point to Deploy to Vercel buttons and a quick-start flow
• Category navigation – table of contents lets you scan by use case instead of hunting through random demos
• MIT license – repo metadata and README both mark it as MIT licensed for reuse and adaptation

It’s open-source (MIT license).

Link in the reply 👇

Andrej Karpathy explains why RLHF needed a cheat code.

The naive version sounds simple: show humans model outputs and ask them to score which ones are good.

Then he does the math:

"I want to do 1,000 updates, where each update will be on 1,000 prompts, and for each prompt we're going to have 1,000 rollouts that we're scoring."

"I will need to ask a human to evaluate a joke a total of 1 billion times."

"That's a lot of people looking at really terrible jokes, so we don't want to do that."

The trick is indirection:

"We're going to involve humans just a little bit, and the way we cheat is that we basically train a whole separate neural network that we call a reward model."

That is the hidden abstraction behind RLHF:

Humans do not scale the feedback loop.
A model trained to imitate human preference scales it.

The biggest risk in AI is concentration: of power, capabilities and economic wealth. Who can doubt it with trillion dollar companies and government now already controlling a massive part of it?

So we need more rebels and more rebel alliance like this one from @usv and friends. Beautiful message!

🎯 Limitations:

RL finetuning is necessary for stability but sacrifices motion diversity enabled by diffusion motion generator. Motion generation on-the-fly is challenging at OOD cases and can still lead to infeasible (physically or untrackable) references.
